KO_H01: 보통 사람들이 다른 사람의 권리를 존중하게 되는 이유 중 하나는 국가나 법 같은 더 높은 권력이 존재하기 때문이다. 그 권력이 규칙을 만들고, 규칙을 어기면 제재를 가한다. 그런데 만약 그런 강제적인 힘이 없다면 어떨까? 누가 시키지도 않고, 어기더라도 벌을 받지 않는 상황에서도, 스스로 판단해서 다른 사람의 권리를 침해하지 않으려고 노력하는 것은 사실 매우 어려운 일이다. 왜냐하면 인간은 자신에게 이익이 되는 방향으로 행동하려는 본능이 있기 때문이다. 따라서 외부의 강제 없이도 공정하게 행동한다는 것은 드물고 힘든 일이다.
KO_H02: 혁명이란 기존의 지배자나 억압하던 세력을 무너뜨리고 새로운 세상을 만들려는 격렬한 변화다. 그런데 역사적으로 혁명이 일어나면, 혁명 이전에 적이었던 세력이 오히려 혁명 이후에 새로운 지배자가 되어버리는 일이 자주 발생했다. 즉, 억압받던 사람들이 억압하던 사람들을 몰아내고 혁명에 성공했더니, 결국 그 자리에 다시 새로운 억압자가 들어서는 것이다. 그렇기 때문에, 혁명을 통해 세상을 바꾸려 했을 때 적어도 그런 최악의 결과, 즉 원래의 적이 새로운 지배자로 바뀌는 일만 일어나지 않아도 그 혁명은 어느 정도 성공한 셈이고, 그것만으로도 운이 좋은 것이라고 볼 수 있다.
KO_H03: 자유롭다는 것은 단순히 하고 싶은 것을 마음대로 할 수 있다는 뜻이 아니다. 자유에는 반드시 그에 따른 책임이 따른다. 즉, 내가 스스로 선택하고 행동한 결과에 대해 스스로 책임을 져야 한다는 의미다. 남 탓을 할 수 없고, 누군가가 대신 책임져 주지도 않는다. 그런데 대부분의 사람들은 이 책임을 지는 것이 무겁고 부담스럽다. 책임을 피하기 위해 오히려 자유가 없는 상태, 즉 누군가가 결정해주고 지시해주는 상태를 더 편하게 여기기도 한다. 그래서 많은 사람들이 겉으로는 자유를 원한다고 말하지만, 실제로는 자유가 주는 책임의 무게 때문에 자유를 불편하게 여기거나 두려워한다.
KO_H04: 인간이 살면서 겪는 괴로움, 불안, 갈등, 불행의 근본적인 원인을 하나만 꼽자면, 그것은 혼자 조용히 있는 것을 못 한다는 점이다. 사람들은 혼자 아무것도 하지 않고 고요하게 머물 때 내면의 공허함이나 불안을 느끼기 때문에, 그것을 피하려고 끊임없이 바깥으로 나가거나 무언가를 하거나 다른 사람과 관계를 맺으려 한다. 그 과정에서 욕심, 오해, 충돌, 후회 같은 것들이 생겨나고 결국 불행이 만들어진다. 만약 사람이 혼자 조용히 자기 자신과 함께 있는 법을 알고 그것을 편안하게 느낄 수 있다면, 많은 불행을 피할 수 있을 것이다.
KO_H05: 사람들은 흔히 여행을 멀리 낯선 곳으로 떠나는 것이라고 생각한다. 하지만 진짜 길고 어려운 여행은 외부 세계가 아니라 자기 자신의 내면, 즉 자신의 감정, 욕망, 가치관, 본모습을 탐구하는 과정이다. 자기 자신이 어떤 사람인지, 무엇을 원하는지, 어떻게 살고 싶은지를 스스로 깊이 들여다보고 진정한 자기 자신을 선택하는 것은 매우 힘든 과정이다. 그런데 그 과정을 거쳐 자기 자신을 제대로 이해하고 받아들인 사람만이, 비로소 다른 사람을 진심으로 이해하고 가까워질 수 있는 능력을 갖추게 된다. 자기 자신을 모르는 상태에서는 타인과의 진정한 관계도 불가능하다는 뜻이다.
KO_L01: 오늘 아침에 평소보다 일찍 잠자리에서 일어났다. 일어난 후 따뜻한 물로 얼굴을 씻었다. 세수를 마친 뒤 아침 식사를 했다. 밥을 먹은 다음 학교로 출발해서 학교에서 하루를 보냈다. 수업이 끝난 저녁에 학교에서 집으로 돌아왔다.
KO_L02: 그 사람은 어제 서울에서 기차를 타고 부산까지 이동했다. 부산에 도착한 후 볼일을 마치고, 오늘 다시 기차를 타고 서울로 돌아왔다.
KO_L03: 나는 어제 친구와 약속을 잡아서 만났다. 만난 뒤 둘이 함께 카페에 들어가서 커피를 각자 주문해 마셨다. 커피를 마시는 동안 서로 이야기를 주고받으며 시간을 보냈다.
KO_L04: 주말에 가족 모두 함께 집 근처에 있는 공원으로 외출했다. 공원에 도착한 뒤 가족들과 함께 걸으며 산책을 즐겼다. 걷다가 피곤해지자 공원 안에 있는 벤치에 앉았다. 미리 준비해 온 도시락을 꺼내어 가족들과 함께 나누어 먹었다.
KO_L05: 아침에 집을 나서 지하철을 타고 출근해서 오전 내내 회사에서 일했다. 점심시간이 되자 회사 근처에 있는 식당으로 가서 점심 식사를 했다. 식사를 마친 뒤 회사로 돌아와 오후에도 계속 업무를 이어서 했다.
EN_H01: Right now, many people are gripped by fear — fear of losing their jobs, their savings, and their sense of security during a time of serious national crisis. But the greatest danger we face is not the crisis itself; it is our own response to it. Specifically, it is a kind of fear that cannot be clearly named, that does not follow logic or reason, and that is not backed up by facts — a pure, overwhelming panic. This kind of terror is dangerous not because it reflects a real threat, but because it stops people from thinking clearly and taking the actions that are urgently needed. When people are paralyzed by this irrational panic, they stop trying, pull back from challenges, and give up on solutions — which makes the crisis worse, not better. The only thing truly standing between us and recovery is this self-defeating, irrational fear, because that fear is what prevents us from doing the necessary work of turning a situation that is getting worse into one that is getting better.
EN_H02: If you dedicate yourself to confronting, opposing, or fighting against people or forces that are dangerous, cruel, or deeply evil, you need to watch yourself very carefully, because there is a serious risk that you will slowly become just as dangerous, cruel, or evil as the thing you are fighting against. This is not a sudden change — it happens gradually and without you noticing. Here is why: when you spend a long time staring deeply into something that is profoundly dark, destructive, and morally empty — imagine a bottomless pit of pure darkness — that darkness does not stay passive. It starts to look back at you. The longer you study, engage with, or are surrounded by evil, the more it begins to shape your thoughts, your habits, your values, and your character. Over time, you may find yourself thinking and acting like the very thing you set out to destroy. The warning is this: do not assume that because your cause is good, you are immune to corruption. The act of fighting evil, if done long enough without self-awareness, can turn you into evil.
EN_H03: When a person is given power over other people — meaning the ability to make decisions that affect others, to give orders, or to control resources — that person tends to start behaving in more selfish, dishonest, or harmful ways over time. The more power they have, the worse this moral decline tends to become. When a person holds total, unlimited, unchecked control over others — with no laws, no accountability, and no one able to stop them — this process of moral corruption becomes complete: they become fully and thoroughly bad in how they think and act. This means that people throughout history who are remembered and celebrated as great — great rulers, great military leaders, great thinkers — were almost certainly also responsible for serious moral failures, harm, cruelty, or injustice during their lives. This is true even for people who never held an official title or position of formal authority, but who were still powerful because others listened to them, admired them, or followed their ideas — because that kind of informal social power can corrupt a person's character just as much as official power can.
EN_H04: If you look at all of recorded human history — spanning thousands of years and countless generations — you can observe a very slow overall pattern: over enormous stretches of time, human societies tend to move, bit by bit, in the direction of becoming more fair, more humane, and more just. Think of this as a very long, slowly curving path that eventually bends toward fairness, the way a long road can curve. However, this gradual movement toward justice is not automatic, inevitable, or free. It does not happen simply because time passes. It only happens when a large enough number of ordinary people actively choose to work toward it — by speaking up against injustice, organizing with others, making personal sacrifices, and persistently pushing for change even when it is hard or dangerous to do so. Without that sustained, collective human effort, history does not bend toward justice on its own — it can easily curve toward cruelty, indifference, or oppression instead. So while there is reason to believe a more just world is possible, it is not guaranteed; it must be actively and continuously built by enough people who refuse to remain passive.
EN_H05: When you are in the middle of a genuinely hard, painful, or crisis-level situation — one that feels like a failure or a dead end — that very situation often contains new possibilities and openings that would not have existed if everything were going smoothly. Difficult circumstances can create chances for change, growth, or innovation that were not available before. However, most people fail to notice these opportunities, and the reason is specific: to see them, you must first be willing to fully and honestly admit that whatever plan, approach, or belief you were relying on before has not worked and will not work — and then let go of it completely. This is emotionally and intellectually very hard. The old approach, even though it has failed, feels familiar and safe. You know how it works. You have invested time and effort in it. Abandoning it means stepping into uncertainty, which feels frightening. But as long as you hold onto the failed approach — defending it, tweaking it, hoping it will eventually work — your attention stays locked on the past, and you cannot see the new opportunity that is sitting right in front of you. Recognizing opportunity in difficulty requires the specific courage to accept failure, release certainty, and face the unknown.
EN_L01: This morning — meaning earlier today, at a time earlier than I usually wake up, or earlier than most people typically do — I finished sleeping and got out of the bed where I had been sleeping at my home. I then went to the bathroom and used a toothbrush — a small brush with a handle — coated with toothpaste to clean all the surfaces of my teeth in order to remove bacteria and food residue and keep my teeth healthy. After that, I put on a full set of clothing appropriate for the day. Once I was dressed, I walked to the kitchen — a room in my home specifically equipped for preparing and cooking food — and sat down at the table there, which is a flat raised surface with legs used for eating, and I ate a morning meal to give my body energy for the day. After finishing that meal, I got into my own personal car — a privately owned motor vehicle that runs on fuel and that I am licensed and able to operate — and drove it along public roads to the building or location where I am employed and go to perform my job on working days.
EN_L02: She left wherever she had been — likely her home or the place where she works — and traveled entirely on foot, without using any vehicle, to a small shop that sells various everyday items and that is located at the point where two streets intersect, which was close enough to reach by walking. Once inside the shop, she chose a sealed plastic or glass bottle filled with plain drinking water and a ready-made sandwich — a food item consisting of a filling such as meat, cheese, or vegetables placed between two slices of bread — and paid money to the shopkeeper in exchange for both items. These two items together would serve as her lunch, meaning her midday meal. She then walked to a nearby public park — an outdoor area of land with grass, paths, and seating, open and free for anyone to use — found an unoccupied bench, which is a flat outdoor seat typically made of wood or metal, sat down on it, and ate the food she had just purchased as her meal for the middle of the day.
EN_L03: The day before today — yesterday — during the hours of the afternoon, which is the period between noon and early evening, my brother — a male person who was born to the same parents as me and who lives in a different home from mine — traveled from wherever he lives to my house and entered it to spend time together. The two of us relaxed together on the couch — a long, cushioned piece of furniture in my home designed for sitting or lying down — while watching a movie play on a screen, meaning a full-length fictional or documentary film. When evening arrived and it was time for dinner, the evening meal, rather than preparing food ourselves we used a phone or an internet app to contact a pizza restaurant — a business that makes and delivers food — and placed an order for a pizza to be brought directly to my home. We ate that delivered pizza together as our dinner. As the evening continued and the clock reached approximately 9 o'clock at night — not morning — my brother said goodbye, left my house, and traveled back to his own home.
EN_L04: This morning — earlier today, before noon — I needed to go to the public library, which is a building funded by the local community where any resident can borrow books and other materials for free, for a set period of time, as long as they return them by a specific deadline. Because I traveled by public bus — a large vehicle that carries many passengers along a fixed route in exchange for a small fare — rather than by car or on foot, I waited for and then rode the bus to get there. When I arrived, I handed back three books that I had borrowed from the library on a previous visit. These books had a specific date printed on them or recorded in the library's system by which they were required to be returned — that date had arrived or was very close — so returning them was necessary to avoid a late fee or penalty. While I was at the library, I also looked through the available books and selected two that I had not read before and that I wanted to read. I borrowed them — meaning the library recorded that I had taken them and that I am responsible for returning them by a future due date — and brought them with me. After completing both tasks, I boarded the bus again, rode it back toward my home, and arrived back at my house before the midday meal, meaning before noon.
EN_L05: When my workday ended and I left the building or location where I am employed, instead of going directly home I made a brief stop — meaning I did not stay long — at a grocery store, which is a large retail shop that sells food, drinks, and household supplies. Inside the store, I found and selected a container of milk — a white liquid dairy product that comes from cows and that is commonly kept in a refrigerator — and a carton or package of eggs — oval-shaped foods produced by hens that are used in cooking — because these were items that I needed at home and did not currently have enough of. I paid for them at the checkout and then got into my car and drove along roads back to my home. Once home, I went to the kitchen and prepared and cooked a meal for the evening — dinner — that did not require many ingredients, complex techniques, or a long time to make. After eating, I sat in front of my television — an electronic screen in my home that displays video programs transmitted over broadcast signals or cable — and watched a news program, which is a regularly scheduled show in which journalists and reporters inform viewers about recent events that have happened locally, in the country, and around the world.
